coredata.dc:title: Engineering nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: Nitrogen content-controlled dual-phase emission behavior
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xingchen
Yu, Jingyan
Tan, Yonggen
Zhang, Wengao
Zhu, Lingquan
Ye, Shenglin
Feng, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen doping is a widely used method for enhancing the performance of carbon quantum dots (CQD). However, the precise relationship between nitrogen content and emission spectra remains unclear when preparing high-performance nitrogen-doped CQD (N-CQD). This study systematically investigates the effects of nitrogen content on the crystalline structure, optical properties, and electronic band structure of N-CQD. Citric acid was used as the carbon source, and ethylenediamine monohydrate was used as the nitrogen source, with their ratio controlled to hydrothermal synthesized N-CQD with N/C ratios ranging from 0 to 0.4. Notably, when the N/C ratio increases from 0 to 0.2, the N-CQD exhibits redshifted emission with excitation dependence. However, when the N/C ratio rises from 0.2 to 0.4, the N-CQD shows blueshifted emission with excitation-independence. We define it as the dual-phase emission behavior of N-CQD attributed to the transition of doping sites from graphitic nitrogen to pyridine nitrogen with increased nitrogen content. DFT calculations indicate that different doping sites influence electron transfer in N-CQD, resulting in distinct optical behaviors. Importantly, this work comprehensively explains the relationship between nitrogen content and the emission behavior of N-CQD for the first time, providing crucial insights for refining the theoretical framework of N-CQD.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped
Carbon quantum dots
Doping site
Theoretical calculation
Emission behavior

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The morphology and chemical properties of N-CQD
The effect of nitrogen doping sites in the emission behavior of N-CQD

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge financial support from the Southern University of Science and Technology and the Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory Program under contract No. 2021B1212040001. This work was also supported by the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2021M701589). We also acknowledge the assistance of SUSTech Core Research Facilities. Finally, we acknowledge Professor Chao Zhang from Anhui University of Science and Technology for providing the license to the computing software.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Compositing process and structural characterization of the N-CQD. (a) Schematic diagram of N-CQD synthesis and thin film preparation. (b) XRD patterns of the five types of N-CQD. (c) Raman spectra of the five types of N-CQD. (d) FT-IR spectra of the five types of N-CQD.
Morphologies of the N-CQD. TEM images of G1 (a), G2 (b), G3 (c), G4 (d) and G5 (e). Insets are the corresponding HR-TEM at the bottom right corner, respectively.

(a–e) High-resolution XPS spectra of N 1s spectrum for G1 (a), G2 (b), G3 (c), G4 (d), and G5 (e), respectively. (f) The variation trend of atomic percentages for atoms at pyridine nitrogen doping sites as a function of nitrogen content.

The spectral behavior of N-CQD. (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the five types of N-CQD. (b) PLQY of the five types of N-CQD at 390 nm. (c) Emission spectrum contour plot under excitation from 350 nm to 450 nm in 10 nm intervals of the five types of N-CQD. Insets are the emission figures under 365 nm UV light at the bottom left corner, respectively. (d) The emission wavelengths and emission shift differences of the five types of N-CQD with different N/C ratios at excitation wavelengths of 350 nm, 370 nm, and 390 nm, respectively.

(a) Theoretical models of N-CQD corresponding to the experimental with different nitrogen doping sites. (b–f) The calculated absorption spectra for the theoretical models G1 (b), G2 (c), G3 (d), G4 (e), and G5 (f), respectively. (g) Summary of the trends in absorption intensity and wavelength.
(a) Orbital energy diagrams for pyridine N-CQD, outer-layer graphitic N-CQD, and inner-layer graphitic N-CQD. (b) Molecular orbital diagrams and energy gaps for the G1, G2, G3, G4, and G5 structures.

QY, τ ,
k r
, and
k
nr
of five N-CQD samples with different nitrogen contents.

head.ce:title: Engineering nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: Nitrogen content-controlled dual-phase emission behavior

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
Southern University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen doping is a widely used method for enhancing the performance of carbon quantum dots (CQD). However, the precise relationship between nitrogen content and emission spectra remains unclear when preparing high-performance nitrogen-doped CQD (N-CQD). This study systematically investigates the effects of nitrogen content on the crystalline structure, optical properties, and electronic band structure of N-CQD. Citric acid was used as the carbon source, and ethylenediamine monohydrate was used as the nitrogen source, with their ratio controlled to hydrothermal synthesized N-CQD with N/C ratios ranging from 0 to 0.4. Notably, when the N/C ratio increases from 0 to 0.2, the N-CQD exhibits redshifted emission with excitation dependence. However, when the N/C ratio rises from 0.2 to 0.4, the N-CQD shows blueshifted emission with excitation-independence. We define it as the dual-phase emission behavior of N-CQD attributed to the transition of doping sites from graphitic nitrogen to pyridine nitrogen with increased nitrogen content. DFT calculations indicate that different doping sites influence electron transfer in N-CQD, resulting in distinct optical behaviors. Importantly, this work comprehensively explains the relationship between nitrogen content and the emission behavior of N-CQD for the first time, providing crucial insights for refining the theoretical framework of N-CQD.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQD) have recently developed rapidly [1–7]. Compared with classical semiconductor quantum dots, CQD possesses unique optical, electronic, and chemical properties, such as high photoluminescence, low toxicity, and easy functionalization [8–14]. They have great potential in optoelectronic devices, bioimaging, catalysis, and other fields [15–24]. Unlike classical semiconductor quantum dots, the emission behavior of CQD is governed by both core control and surface modification. Nitrogen doping can modify CQD's surface states and functional groups, providing a crucial method for tuning their optical properties and exploring their emission behaviour [25–33]. Over the past decade, nitrogen-doped CQD (N-CQD) has achieved significant results, including high external quantum efficiency LED devices [18], full-color emission [25], and solid-state fluorescence [26]. However, the relationship between nitrogen content and emission spectra remains unclear when preparing high-performance N-CQD, and the theoretical framework is yet to be established, often leading to conflicting results. Therefore, further systematic research is needed.

There have been many studies about the N-CQD. The current understanding of the emission behavior of N-CQD is that increasing nitrogen content alters electron transfer, leading to redshifted emission [34–38]. Miao et al. observed significant redshift in the synthesized N-CQD with increasing reaction temperature or nitrogen content; they controlled the nitrogen content to achieve full-color emission N-CQD and developed white light LEDs [32]. Meanwhile, numerous theoretical calculations have demonstrated that the incorporation of nitrogen alters electron transport in N-CQD, leading to n-π electron transfer, which causes the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to shift upward and results in redshifted emission [39–42]. However, previous experiments have shown some contradictory results. Yuan et al. performed a secondary reaction on N-CQD to incorporate nitrogen further, synthesizing deep blue-emitting N-CQD with an emission wavelength of 433 nm and fabricating high-efficiency LED devices [17]. Therefore, to advance the further development of N-CQD, it is essential to clarify the relationship between nitrogen content and the emission behavior of these dots.

In this study, citric acid (CA) was used as the carbon source, and ethylenediamine monohydrate (EDA·H2O) was used as the nitrogen source to hydrothermally synthesize N-CQD with an N/C ratio ranging from 0 to 0.4. From undoped, underdoped to overdoped, we aim to systematically investigate how different nitrogen content impact the emission behavior of N-CDQ. We found that as the nitrogen content increases, the basicity of the nitrogen source accelerates the dehydration of the acidic carbon source, leading to rapid carbon core formation and enhanced graphitization. Therefore, at lower nitrogen content, the carbon core forms more slowly, allowing nitrogen atoms to be incorporated into the carbon core as graphite nitrogen, which generates defect states and additional electronic states. This results in non-radiative transitions and an upward shift of the HOMO. Consequently, N-CQD exhibits weaker photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY) and photoluminescence (PL) lifetime, redshifted emission, and excitation-dependent behavior under low nitrogen content. As the nitrogen content gradually increases, the formation of the carbon core accelerates, and nitrogen atoms preferentially incorporate on the carbon core surface as pyridine nitrogen. The intact carbon core reduces non-radiative transitions and suppresses additional electronic states, causing the HOMO to shift downward. Consequently, under overdoped, N-CQD exhibits significantly enhanced PLQY and PL lifetime, blueshifted emission, and excitation-independent behavior. Additionally, to complement our experimental findings and characterization, we employed advanced modeling techniques to investigate the structure and electronic and optical properties of N-CQD. This research provides an in-depth understanding of how nitrogen content influences carbon core formation, nitrogen doping sites, optical properties, and N-CQD emission behavior, contributing to this versatile nanomaterial's controllable synthesis and future development.

Citric acid (CA) is purchased from Macklin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Ethylenediamine monohydrate (EDA·H2O) is purchased from Tixiai (Shanghai) Chemical Industry Development Co., Ltd. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (Mw = 67000) is purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Deionized water was used during the synthesis process. All the chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used as received.

We set five types of N-CQD that divide the N/C ratio. The N-CQD was synthesized by hydrothermal process. CA (0.1 g) was dissolved in deionized water (30 ml), and 0, 14.12, 31.76, 54.45, and 84.69 ul EDA·H2O was added to ensure that the N/C ratio is 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, and 0.4, respectively. After one hour of sonication, the clear precursor solution was then transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (50 ml) and heated at 180 °C for four hours. After the reaction, the solution was cooled to room temperature, and the N-CQD was freeze-dried to get the powder. The samples with N/C ratios of 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, and 0.4 were named G1, G2, G3, G4, and G5, respectively.

N-CQD films were mixed with a PVA substrate solution and uniformly coated on quartz glass by spinning coating. 1 g PVA was first dispersed in 10 g of cool water for two hours; then, the mixture was heated at 70 °C in a water bath for 30 min to dissolve the PVA fully. Finally, 0.01 g N-CQD was added to the PVA solution under vigorous stirring. Here, the resulting homogenous solution was used for spin coating. PVA/N-CQD aqueous solution was dripped onto a one-inch glass sheet, then rotated at 1500 rpm (acceleration 500 rpm/s) for 10 s, and N-CQD film was obtained after drying. The N-CQD/PVA film is used solely in Fig. 1 (a) to illustrate the emission behavior of the prepared N-CQD and is not related to subsequent experimental characterizations.

The subsequent fluorescence spectrum, lifetime, and quantum yield were measured using the FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) were shown with a Smartlab X-ray diffractometer at the angle from 10 to 80 degrees (scan speed 10 degrees/min, scan step 0.01 degree, RIGAKU, Japan). Raman spectra were collected on Lab RAM HR Raman microscope (Horiba, Japan). The microstructure and particle size distribution were recorded using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Tecnai, F30, 300 KeV, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured on PHI 5000 Versaprobe (ULVAC-PHI, Japan). The XPS spectra were calibrated to an adventitious carbon peak at 284.8 eV. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–vis spectra) were measured on Lambda 750 s (PerkinElmer, USA), and the UV–vis spectra were also recorded to cross-check the XPS analysis results. The Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR spectra) of solid-state samples (KBr matrix) were measured on a Vertex 70 V spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) in the region 1000–4000 cm−1. The materials used in all characterization in this work are N-CQD powders or solutions.

Quantum yield values were determined using the FLS1000 Fluorescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) equipped with an integrating sphere, using a 1 cm pathlength quartz cuvette. Both excitation and emission slit widths were set to 3 nm. The excitation wavelength was set to 390 nm, and the fluorescence spectrum was acquired from 380 nm to 700 nm. Triplicate scans were acquired with a dwell time of 0.1 s. The data were processed using the software supplied by the manufacturer.

The theoretical calculations used a self-built workstation with two Intel Xeon 8373C processors with 144 cores. Spin unrestricted DFT calculations calculate the geometric optimization and property of N-CQD, performed using the Dmol3 code, with numerical functions on an atom-centered grid as their atomic basis [43,44]. The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) method is utilized as the exchange–correlation functional [45]. The DFT-D method within the Grimme scheme is adopted to describe the van der Waals (vdW) interaction [46]. The convergence tolerance of energy is 1.0 e-5 Ha; the maximum force is 0.002 Ha/Å, and the maximum displacement is 0.005 Å in geometry optimization. Finally, transient density functional theory (TDDFT) was employed to calculate 25 excited states and the electronic absorption spectra for each structure. The potential function used in these calculations was the SAOP model potential. The obtained absorption spectra were subjected to Gaussian broadening with a broadening coefficient of 100 to simulate ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology and chemical properties of N-CQD
ce:section.ce:para: To systematically investigate how different nitrogen content impacts the emission behavior of N-CDQ, we designed experiments and synthesized N-CQD from undoped, underdoped, and overdoped. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), CA and EDA·H2O were uniformly mixed and subjected to hydrothermal synthesis, green and efficient synthesis N-CQD with N/C ratio ranging from 0 to 0.4 (named as G1 (N/C = 0), G2 (N/C = 0.1), G3 (N/C = 0.2), G4 (N/C = 0.3), and G5 (N/C = 0.4), respectively). These N-CQD with different nitrogen doping levels exhibit distinct emission spectra under 365 nm UV light, and the underlying reasons require further investigation. Table S1 (Supporting Information) provides detailed data and calculation methods. To verify the successful preparation of the N-CQD, the crystal structure and morphology were characterized by X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD), Raman spectra, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR spectra), and transmission electron microscope (TEM).

The XRD patterns of G1, G2, G3, G4, and G5 exhibit a broad peak at 21°, with a rightward shift to 25.4° as the nitrogen content increases (Fig. 1(b)), which is attributed to the graphitic structure with interlayer spacing (0 0 2) of 0.42 and 0.35 nm according to Bragg's Law. The broad peak at 21°, corresponding to a d-spacing of 0.42 nm, is primarily attributed to the increased interlayer distance. This enlargement arises from the distortion of the graphene plane due to the presence of sp3 C [32]. The peak at 25.4° is the characteristic (002) diffraction peak of graphite. Its appearance indicates that the interlayer stacking within N-CQD is very close to that in graphite (≈0.35 nm) and suggests that the highly conjugate sp2 domain dominates within the core part of N-CQD. This indicates that the graphitization degree of N-CQD increases with nitrogen content. Notably, without nitrogen doping, the XRD pattern of the G1 sample exhibits a mixed phase of citric acid crystalline and amorphous structures (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). This indicates that the carbon core forms more slowly in the undoped state. As the doping level increases, the formation of the carbon core accelerates, with an increased degree of graphitization, which is further confirmed by the Raman spectra presented below.

Typical Raman spectra of the five types of N-CQD are shown in Fig. 1(c). The G band at 1585 cm−1 and the D band at 1365 cm−1 are designated as indicative of graphite clusters and disordered carbon structures, respectively [47]. The intensity ratios of the D peak to the G peak (ID/IG) for these five samples were calculated. As nitrogen doping increases, the ID/IG value continuously decreases from 0.928 to 0.682, indicating that the degree of graphitization of the N-CQD improves, consistent with the XRD results.

Fig. 1(d) shows the FT-IR spectrum of N-CQD, providing evidence of characteristic nitrogen and oxygen functional groups, which confirms the successful nitrogen doping of the N-CQD. The G1 sample exhibits a distinct CO stretching vibration peak at 1691 cm−1 in the undoped state. With the increase in nitrogen content, a significant peak shift is observed. Peaks at 1651 cm−1 and 1535 cm−1 emerge prominently, corresponding to the stretching vibration of the CN bond and the CC stretching vibration in the benzene ring, respectively [18,37]. It confirms the successful incorporation of nitrogen and, under nitrogen's influence, the carbon core's accelerated formation, supporting the viewpoint mentioned above.

Fig. 2 (a–e) shows the TEM and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images of G1, G2, G3, G4, and G5, respectively. The five types of N-CQD exhibit a spherical shape and have very similar particle sizes, and the images illustrate that all these N-CQD have highly crystalline carbon structures. Two kinds of lattice fringe distances were observed, around 0.32 nm and 0.22 nm, attributed to the d spacings of the graphite (001) and graphene (100) planes. This indicates that with the increase in nitrogen content, the degree of graphitization of N-CQD increases, and the internal hybridization transitions from a mixed state of sp2 and sp3 to a predominantly sp2-hybridized structure. Which causes the transition from graphite lattice to graphene lattice [32,37,48].

Furthermore, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) can help study the valence band structure and elemental site distribution of N-CQD. The binding energy scale was calibrated using the C1s standard value of 284.8 eV. The full XPS spectra ( Fig. S2 , Supporting Information) reveal three characteristic binding energies: C 1s at 286 eV, O 1s at 532 eV, and N 1s at 400 eV, and the ratio of N to C changes from 0 to 0.235, respectively, indicating the increase of nitrogen content.

The deconvolution of the high-resolution C 1s and O 1s spectra are shown in Figs. S3 and S4 (Supporting Information). For C 1s, XPS spectra can be converted into CC/CC at 284.8 eV, CO, and CN at 286.3 eV, OCO at 288.7 eV, and CO at 288.0 eV. For O 1s, XPS spectra can be converted into CO at 530.9 eV and CO at 532.4 eV. The high-resolution N 1s spectra in Fig. 3 (a–e) fitted with two Gaussian peaks: pyridine nitrogen (399.8 eV) and graphitic nitrogen (400.7 eV). This XPS data analysis aligns well with the mentioned FT-IR characterization.

The variation trend of atomic percentages for atoms at pyridine nitrogen doping sites was reported as a function of nitrogen content (Fig. 3(f)), and the relative content of pyridine N increases to 81 % as nitrogen content increases to N/C = 0.4. Graphitic nitrogen corresponds to doping within the core, while pyridine nitrogen primarily represents surface nitrogen bonds. The proportion of pyridine nitrogen increases with higher nitrogen content, confirming that the carbon precursor is supplied more rapidly at elevated nitrogen levels. As a result, nitrogen atoms are less likely to incorporate into the N-CQD core and tend to bind on the surface.

Based on the structural characterization of N-CQD described above, it can be concluded that CA and EDA·H2O compete to facilitate the growth of C and N atoms. EDA·H2O is a diamine compound containing two fundamental amino groups (NH2), while CA is acidic, containing carboxyl (COOH) and hydroxyl groups (OH). When the amount of EDA·H2O increases, it promotes the dehydration of CA, forming lactone or ester products, thereby accelerating the formation of the carbon core. Higher nitrogen content accelerates carbon core formation, enhances the graphitization degree of N-CQD, reduces graphitic nitrogen incorporation in the core, and promotes surface pyridine nitrogen [47,48]. This demonstrates the ability to control doping levels and surface chemistry by adjusting the nitrogen content.

The optical properties of these five types of N-CQD were characterized, including their Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–vis spectra), emission spectra, PLQY, and PL lifetime. The UV–vis spectra of the five types of N-CQD were characterized in a 10 mm quartz cuvette using deionized water as a reference, and the corresponding spectra for different N-CQD are given in Fig. 4
(a), notably, with the increase in nitrogen content, N-CQD transition from a peakless for the G1 sample to a multi-peak (341 nm, 433 nm, 457 nm) for the G3 sample and then to a single peak (347 nm) for the G5 sample. The absorption band at 340–350 nm corresponds to the n-π* transition of the CN bond, with peak intensity increasing as nitrogen content rises. Meanwhile, the 430–460 nm peak emerges upon nitrogen doping but weakens with higher nitrogen content, corresponding to the defect states and π-π* transition associated with graphitic nitrogen doping sites. As the nitrogen content increases, the proportion of graphitic nitrogen decreases, resulting in a decline in absorption peak intensity around 450 nm. The variations in absorption bands and peak positions indicate the regulatory effect of nitrogen content on the electronic structure and chemical environment of N-CQD. Furthermore, if we further refine the classification of the N/C ratio, the same behavior will be observed (Fig. S5, Supporting Information).

The PL properties of all N-CQD samples were investigated. PLQY at 390 nm excitation wavelength of the five samples is depicted in Fig. 4(b) and increases exponentially with the rise in nitrogen content, the highest QY reaching 62.4 % for the G5 sample with the N/C = 0.4. As the nitrogen doping content increases, its PL lifetime grows from 6.36 ns to 14.78 ns (Fig. S6, Supporting Information). In general, the luminescence process of any material includes both radiative and non-radiative transitions. When electrons absorb energy and enter the excited state, if energy is released by emitting photons, it is a radiative transition; if energy is released as heat through vibrations or other means, it is a non-radiative transition. Materials with high PLQY generally indicate weak non-radiative transitions, with energy primarily released in the form of photons. By measuring PLQY and PL lifetime, we can gain insight into the relative strength of radiative and non-radiative transitions to some extent. The
k r
(
radiative rate constants) and
k
nr
(non-radiative rate constants) for samples G1, G2, G3, G4, and G5 were calculated using the following formulas: [49,50]
(1)
k r
= Q Y / τ
(2)
k
nr
=
( 1 - Q Y )
/ τ

The calculated results for the five N-CQD samples with different nitrogen contents are shown in Table 1 . The data show that the
k r
increases with increasing nitrogen content, while the
k
nr
shows the opposite trend. The G5 sample, with the highest nitrogen doping level, has the highest
k r
of 4.22*10-2 and the lowest
k
nr
of 2.54*10-2. This can be attributed to the rapid formation of the carbon core, enhanced structural integrity, reduced internal graphite nitrogen substitution and defect states, and lower non-radiative decay rates in high-nitrogen-content N-CQD, leading to increased PL lifetime and PLQY as the nitrogen content rises.

As shown in Fig. 4(c), The diluted N-CQD solutions (0.1 mg/mL) were excited at varying wavelengths from 350 to 450 nm in 10 nm intervals, and the resulting data were visualized as a contour map. Interestingly, under identical precursor conditions, varying nitrogen content caused the synthesized N-CQD to exhibit both excitation-dependent and excitation-independent behavior simultaneously. For samples G2 (N/C = 0.1) and G3 (N/C = 0.2), as the excitation wavelength changes from 350 nm to 450 nm, the emission wavelength shifts by more than 70 nm, redshifting from the blue region to the green region, demonstrating a pronounced excitation-dependent emission behavior. However, sample G5 (N/C = 0.4) exhibits excitation-independent behavior, with a consistent emission peak at 446 nm. The highest PL peak at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm and decreases markedly beyond λex = 400 nm. For a clearer comparison, corresponding to Fig. 4(c), Fig. S7 (Supporting Information) shows the emission images and wavelength shifts of the G2, G3, G4, and G5 samples under 350 nm and 390 nm excitation light, respectively.

In summary, as the nitrogen content increases, the emission spectra of N-CQD exhibit a dual response (Fig. 4(d)), referred to as the dual-phase emission behavior. This phenomenon provides an explanation for the contradictory properties reported in previous studies [17,34–42]. Under high-temperature reaction conditions, the amino groups in the nitrogen source undergo a dehydration condensation reaction with CA, accelerating the formation of the carbon core [47,48]. At lower nitrogen content levels, the degree of CA dehydration condensation is low, resulting in a slower carbon core formation rate. Nitrogen atoms are incorporated into the core, forming graphitic nitrogen doping and some defect states. This results in additional electronic states within the N-CQD and stronger non-radiative transitions. In this state, which we refer to as phase 1, the emission peak redshifts, displaying excitation-dependent behavior with weak emission. As the nitrogen content increases, the reaction enters phase 2, where excessive nitrogen doping promotes complete dehydration condensation of CA, leading to rapid carbon core formation. In this condition, the energy threshold for nitrogen incorporation into the carbon core increases during the reaction, resulting in nitrogen atoms distributed on the carbon core surface, forming pyridine nitrogen. Consequently, the central and defect states of the N-CQD are reduced. Under these conditions, the emission peak blueshifts as nitrogen content rises, showing excitation-independent behavior with strong emission. These findings reveal that nitrogen content affects the emission behavior of N-CQD by modifying the nitrogen doping sites. Understanding these relationships enables more precise tailoring of synthesis processes and optimization of the optical properties of N-CQD for specific applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of nitrogen doping sites in the emission behavior of N-CQD
ce:section.ce:para:
We performed DFT computational modeling to investigate further the influence of nitrogen doping sites on bandgap and work function. Because of the complex structures, CQD is commonly represented as finite graphene fragments, with defects or elemental doping introduced during model construction. Considering the balance between computational speed and model size in Fig. S8 (Supporting Information), we selected a three-layer aromatic ring structure with 72 atoms as the carbon core for the CQD. Based on the experimentally characterized nitrogen distribution (Fig. 3(f)), we constructed five models shown in Fig. 5
(a), which represent the five samples with different nitrogen doping sites. Furthermore, calculations reveal that this doping sequence adheres to the principle of minimum energy, the binding energies of graphitic nitrogen and pyridine nitrogen to be 4.10 eV and 5.51 eV, respectively. This result indicates that the formation of graphitic nitrogen involves a relatively lower energy barrier and is thus preferentially incorporated, which justifies the rationality of this doping sequence from an energy perspective. Fig. S9 (Supporting Information) provides detailed models and calculation parameters.

In Fig. 5(b–f), we illustrate the absorption spectra of N-CQD with varying nitrogen doping site distributions. The results reveal that the location of nitrogen doping sites profoundly influences the position and number of absorption peaks in N-CQD. In the undoped G1 structure, the carbon core remains intact without defects or substitutions, preventing non-radiative transitions and resulting in a single absorption peak with high intensity. In contrast, for the G2 and G3 structures, incorporating graphite nitrogen into the carbon core disrupts its integrity, enhancing non-radiative transitions. This leads to increased absorption peaks, reduced intensity, and significant redshift. Meanwhile, the G5 structure, dominated by pyridine nitrogen located at the edges of the carbon core, exhibits fewer absorption peaks with higher intensity and a blueshift.

As summarized in Fig. 5(g), it can be observed that with the nitrogen doping sites varying, the absorption spectra of N-CQD exhibit two distinct phases, aligning with the experimental absorption spectra obtained earlier. Additionally, we calculated the relationship between nitrogen doping quantity and absorption spectra, as shown in Fig. S10 (Supporting Information), confirming that the absorption spectra are weakly dependent on the doping quantity and are primarily governed by the doping sites.

The analysis of electron density and molecular orbitals offers detailed insights into this dual-phase behavior. Nitrogen, carrying more electrons than carbon, generates electron-dense regions at doping sites when integrated into N-CQD (Fig. S11, Supporting Information). These regions lead to the formation of new electronic states at the transition centers of N-CQD, especially for graphite nitrogen embedded within the carbon core.

Fig. 6
(a) displays molecular orbital calculations for N-CQD doped with pyridine and graphitic nitrogen. For graphitic nitrogen doping, the electron-dense region resides within the carbon core, readily inducing defect states and additional electronic states, promoting π-π* electron transitions. This upward shift of the HOMO level decreases the energy gap, leading to multiple absorption peaks, redshifted emissions, and excitation-dependent luminescence, as described earlier. With pyridine nitrogen doping, the electron-dense region is located at the carbon core edge, preserving its structural integrity. The influence of graphitic nitrogen diminishes, leading to an increased energy gap, blueshifted emissions, fewer absorption peaks, and excitation-independent luminescence.

In Fig. 6(b), we computed the energy gaps of the five structures, showing an initial decrease followed by an increase. This pattern corresponds to emission wavelengths that redshift initially and blueshift subsequently, consistent with experimental observations and the molecular orbital analysis presented before.

In this research, CA and EDA·H2O were used as precursors to investigate the emission behavior trends and electronic mechanisms of N-CQD with varying nitrogen content (N/C ratio ranging from 0 to 0.4). The findings reveal that N-CQD exhibits dual-phase emission behavior as the nitrogen content increases. Specifically, at lower nitrogen content (N/C < 0.2), the dehydration of CA is slower, leading to an incomplete carbon core structure. Nitrogen is doped as graphitic nitrogen within the carbon core, resulting in non-radiative transitions and central electronic states. Consequently, N-CQD generally shows weaker PLQY and PL lifetime, redshifted emissions, and excitation-dependent behavior at low nitrogen doping levels. In contrast, at higher nitrogen content (N/C > 0.2), the carbon core forms more rapidly due to the accelerated dehydration of citric acid, resulting in a structurally complete core. Nitrogen is primarily doped as pyridine nitrogen at the carbon core edges, leading to strong PLQY and PL lifetime, blueshifted emissions, and excitation-independent behavior.

In summary, this study provides an in-depth understanding of how nitrogen content influences carbon core formation, nitrogen doping sites, optical properties, and emission behavior of N-CQD. Additionally, it offers guidance for refining the theoretical framework of N-CQD, providing valuable insights into selecting appropriate nitrogen content to optimize PLQY and emission wavelength and regulating the optical properties of N-CQD. These findings can guide the future design and controlled synthesis of N-CQD, paving the way for broader applications.

Xingchen Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Software, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jingyan Yu: Visualization, Methodology. Yonggen Tan: Investigation, Formal analysis. Wengao Zhang: Supervision. Lingquan Zhu: Writing – original draft. Shenglin Ye: Writing – review & editing. Jun Feng: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from the Southern University of Science and Technology and the Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory Program under contract No. 2021B1212040001. This work was also supported by the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2021M701589). We also acknowledge the assistance of SUSTech Core Research Facilities. Finally, we acknowledge Professor Chao Zhang from Anhui University of Science and Technology for providing the license to the computing software.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2025.02.024.

ce:section.ce:para:
